messages.author: Mittens
messages.text: I'm working on developing a new open source device for communication and navigation. One key feature I'm trying to achieve is voice over LoRa. I'm very close to having this working, I've been able to record, encode, transmit, receive, decode and play bits of voice packets, but I am currently pretty stuck getting a consistent/steady audio stream. I think I need of an expert in SX12XX LoRa to get the code right. Please message me if you think you or anyone you know could help!
messages.content_html: I'm working on developing a new open source device for communication and navigation. One key feature I'm trying to achieve is voice over LoRa. I'm very close to having this working, I've been able to record, encode, transmit, receive, decode and play bits of voice packets, but I am currently pretty stuck getting a consistent/steady audio stream. I think I need of an expert in SX12XX LoRa to get the code right. Please message me if you think you or anyone you know could help!
messages.reactions_html:
1
1

messages.author: RQ (darco)
messages.text: I’ll just say that I’m not an SX12XX expert, but I’m super curious how your example works. I assume it is half duplex? Is it real-time or just recordings? What audio codec are you using? Is this a 2.4GHz feature or something you want to do over traditional LoRa frequencies?

messages.author: Mike WB6KTN
messages.text: You will likely need to implement a protocol on top of the physical layer with additional FEC to support streaming audio over an unreliable channel with no feedback. You will likely need to update the channel loss model to factor in LoRa's chirp modulation and everything LoRa already does to spread the FEC symbols across the packet to mitigate burst loss. It is a non-trivial problem. Loss in a low bit rate codec is devistating. Codecs that can deliver understandable voice in the presence of loss can only do so by having unneeded information that can be lost without impacting understandibility. There is nothing special about these chips. Give them a packet, wait for the interrupt. Wait for a packet and add it to the queue. The receive jitter buffer takes some effort but that is independent of the device. background: implemented a dvb/mbms streaming protocol in a former life.

messages.author: Mittens
messages.text: I'm using 'Codec2' since I've found that's able to compress 320 audio samples down to just 8bytes of data (in 1600bps mode) So as to transmit 8kHz sample-rate audio should only need to send 200bytes each second over LoRa sub GHz, which, as far as I'm aware, is possible

messages.author: Mittens
messages.text: I will have to look into some of those concepts you mention. From further investigating my system, my main issue right now seems to first be getting the 200byte per second transfer happening between SX12XX chips in the same room. As I was setting up the audio hardware and encoding I was just using polling on a loop to get messages, but I've found that to have really sketchy reliability. Which makes sense because it's basically random chance if you happen to start listening for a message at the moment the transmitting radio fires out the data. So for now I'm trying to move from polling to interrupts. In other news I've ordered the components from LCSC and boards from PCBWay to build the first hardware prototypes
messages.content_html: I will have to look into some of those concepts you mention. From further investigating my system, my main issue right now seems to first be getting the 200byte per second transfer happening between SX12XX chips in the same room. As I was setting up the audio hardware and encoding I was just using polling on a loop to get messages, but I've found that to have really sketchy reliability. Which makes sense because it's basically random chance if you happen to start listening for a message at the moment the transmitting radio fires out the data. So for now I'm trying to move from polling to interrupts. In other news I've ordered the components from LCSC and boards from PCBWay to build the first hardware prototypes

It is a non-trivial problem. Loss in a low bit rate codec is devistating. messages.author: Mike WB6KTN
messages.text: So for now I'm trying to move from polling to interrupts. That should help. The transmit engine should process a descriptor list transmitting all queued data. The receive engine should processor a descriptor list filling the buffers with received data. This can be done with polling but the timing can get tricky.
messages.content_html:
So for now I'm trying to move from polling to interrupts.
That should help. The transmit engine should process a descriptor list transmitting all queued data. The receive engine should processor a descriptor list filling the buffers with received data. This can be done with polling but the timing can get tricky.

messages.author: RQ (darco)
messages.text: I realize you might not care about WiFi that much, but your existing layout is going to de-tune your WiFi antenna significantly unless you are using a module that has an external antenna. Mind those keepout areas, and keep the copper out of them as much as you can. Sometimes you have to compromise, in that case do something like this and at least keep all copper out. Otherwise really cool! (edited)
messages.content_html: I realize you might not care about WiFi that much, but your existing layout is going to de-tune your WiFi antenna significantly unless you are using a module that has an external antenna. Mind those keepout areas, and keep the copper out of them as much as you can. Sometimes you have to compromise, in that case do something like this and at least keep all copper out. Otherwise really cool! (edited)

messages.text: yeah, not a lot of processing power overhead to use on a microcontroller. Have you tried achieving a continuous stream in your app?
messages.content_html: yeah, not a lot of processing power overhead to use on a microcontroller. Have you tried achieving a continuous stream in your app?

messages.author: bluebrolly.
messages.text: I didnt try it. It can work in theory but if you do that you flood the airways and can cause issues. For example if both are transmitting at the same time, neither will get it because no one is in RX mode, so you would pretty much need to stop a user from sending voice data until no one is sending voice data. Then what happens when you have 2 people talking and 1 person listening, packet loss, collisions, ect. At scale I dont think it would work but point to point with a fast datarate, it should work fine. If you wait a while longer, Semtech v4 LoRA chipsets will be in the 1.6Mbits speeds and you have 2.4MHz LoRA now that you could make a walkie talkie style device. For me, I am going to treat voice with sub ghz lora like uploading a file, record, filter, compress and send once complete.

If you wait a while longer, Semtech v4 LoRA chipsets will be in the 1.6Mbits speeds and you have 2.4MHz LoRA now that you could make a walkie talkie style device. messages.author: Mittens
messages.text: yeah after building the button board, I'm thinking it may not be the right choice, the switches take a lot of force to click, which prevents fast messaging. I don't want to use the touch on the display because it's not a capacitive digitiser, it's got a fair amount of noise and I think it would be irritating having key presses miss often

messages.author: Mittens
messages.text: Yeah, some of the examples I've watched on youtube seem to suffer from that, but that's because they are running each button as an independent binary signal. If they programmed the micro-controller timing the discharge of each pad then you could have an analog scale of how different the capacitance is from baseline, then software could be written to decide on a single likely touch point where there's a cluster of signals, weighting it by the strength. That must be how phone screen digitizers work given their remarkable precicion
messages.content_html: Yeah, some of the examples I've watched on youtube seem to suffer from that, but that's because they are running each button as an independent binary signal. If they programmed the micro-controller timing the discharge of each pad then you could have an analog scale of how different the capacitance is from baseline, then software could be written to decide on a single likely touch point where there's a cluster of signals, weighting it by the strength. That must be how phone screen digitizers work given their remarkable precicion

messages.content_html: Could implement noise suppress to fix this. Only one person can “talk” at a time.
messages.reply_html:
bluebrolly.
I didnt try it. It can work in theory but if you do that you flood the airways and can cause issues. For example if both are transmitting at the same time, neither will get it because no one is in RX mode, so you would pretty much need to stop a user from sending voice data until no one is sending voice data. Then what happens when you have 2 people talking and 1 person listening, packet loss, collisions, ect. At scale I dont think it would work but point to point with a fast datarate, it should work fine. If you wait a while longer, Semtech v4 LoRA chipsets will be in the 1.6Mbits speeds and you have 2.4MHz LoRA now that you could make a walkie talkie style device. For me, I am going to treat voice with sub ghz lora like uploading a file, record, filter, compress and send once complete.
